front: Building: stainless steel catenary arch in St. Louis, Missouri; the world's tallest arch at 630 feet; built as the Jefferson Expansion Memorial
Building: designed as a 'weighted inverted catenary'; the same catenary curves inspired the designer's Dulles Airport roof
Building: Lillian Swann Saarinen designed a reflecting pool surrounded by buffalo originally paired with her husband's catenary curve design for this memorial

back: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (Eero Saarinen)
work: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (1962)

front: Building: sculptural terminal at JFK International Airport, now the TWA Hotel; thin-shell concrete exterior shaped like a bird / 'gullwing-shaped'
back: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (Eero Saarinen)
work: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (1962)

front: Building: all 486 nontempered glass panels had different shapes; writer Vanessa R. Schwartz argues it was inspired by the concrete shell at Disney's Tomorrowland
back: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (Eero Saarinen)
work: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (1962)

front: Building: critic Vincent Scully described this building's 'dramatic patience' and 'willful forcing' as 'paramilitary dandyism'
back: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (Eero Saarinen)
work: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (1962)

back: MIT Chapel and Kresge Auditorium (Eero Saarinen)

front: Building: nondenominational cylindrical brick chapel at MIT; Harry Bertoia sculpture cascades light from a skylight to a white marble altar
Building: MIT Chapel features an aluminum bell tower and spire by sculptor Theodore Roszak
Building: auditorium at MIT shaped like a leaf — a perfect eighth of a sphere — whose opening premiered Aaron Copland's Canticle of Freedom
Building: modernist residence in Columbus, Indiana credited with popularizing the 'conversation pit' — a sunken seating area
Building: four wings stem from a central conversation pit; interiors by Alexander Girard; gardens by Dan Kiley; based on Villa Rotunda
Building: contains a bas relief by sculptor Jacques Lipchitz; located in a city made famous by J. Irwin Miller's architectural patronage
Building: the 2017 film Columbus by Kogonada is set among the modernist architecture of this building's Indiana city
Building: New York skyscraper nicknamed 'The Black Rock' for its dark granite pier facade; designed to be 'the simplest skyscraper in New York City'
Building: Washington D.C.-area airport featuring an airfoil-like concrete roof with catenary curves; designer also created a 'mobile lounge' vehicle for it

back: Ingalls Rink ('Yale Whale') at Yale University (Eero Saarinen)
work: Ingalls Rink ('Yale Whale') at Yale University (1958)

front: Building: ice hockey rink at Yale University nicknamed 'the whale' for its dramatic sloping roof
back: Ingalls Rink ('Yale Whale') at Yale University (Eero Saarinen)
work: Ingalls Rink ('Yale Whale') at Yale University (1958)

front: Building: previously used as an all-girls dormitory with a drawbridge installed to keep men out

work: Ingalls Rink ('Yale Whale') at Yale University (1958)

front: Design: designed the Grasshopper, Womb, and Tulip chairs for the Knoll furniture company
work: Tulip, Womb, and Grasshopper Chairs (for Knoll)

front: Design: Tulip Chair appeared on the original Star Trek set; designed to eliminate the 'slum of legs'
work: Tulip, Womb, and Grasshopper Chairs (for Knoll)

front: Design: Tulip Chair has a fiberglass shell attached to a single aluminum stem, designed to look like they are the same material
work: Tulip, Womb, and Grasshopper Chairs (for Knoll)

front: Design: Florence Knoll collaborated with this designer to produce the Tulip Chair; Knoll named for Florence's married surname
work: Tulip, Womb, and Grasshopper Chairs (for Knoll)

front: Architect: son of Finnish architect Eliel Saarinen; collaborated with him on the General Motors Technical Center in Warren, Michigan
Architect: father's runner-up design for the 1922 Chicago Tribune Tower competition influenced the American Radiator Building in New York
Architect: father was the first director of the Cranbrook Academy of Art, strongly influenced by the Bauhaus
Architect: collaborated with Charles Eames on the Entenza House (Case Study House adjacent to the Eames House)
Architect: designed corporate headquarters for General Motors, John Deere, IBM, and CBS
Architect: designed the Vivian Beaumont Theater at Lincoln Center
Building: Kleinhans Music Hall in Buffalo, New York, designed with Eliel Saarinen; contains the Mary Seaton room
Building: North Christian Church in Columbus, Indiana — hexagonal base with a central metal spire
Building: designed a War Memorial in Milwaukee now housing the Milwaukee Art Museum, to which Calatrava later added the Quadracci Pavilion
Architect: designed the IBM egg-shaped pavilion at the 1964 World's Fair with Charles and Ray Eames
